summary:
The latent heat of vaporization refers to the specific quantity of thermal energy needed for converting saturated liquid at standard conditions directly into gas form; this process occurs with constant temperature during which all added heat goes towards breaking molecular bonds instead of increasing kinetic energies leading up until full evaporation completion from initial state being The summary should focus only on these two lines:

To convert boiling water into steam, additional energy must break the attractive forces between water molecules, known as *latent* heat because once converted, there is no more significant increase in temperature—this term fits describing how much heat changes liquids like water permanently turning gases under normal atmospheric pressure settings typically encountered daily life scenarios such residential kitchens industrial manufacturing facilities power plants etc... \[Option A] Latent Heat Of Vaporisation correctly identifies what type of thermodynamic property quantifies exactly those units measure defining precise amounts thermal input demanded physically transforming substances states while retaining identical temperatures steady-state condition persisting till complete phase shift completed indicating proper choice among available alternatives listed explicitly stated requirement fulfilled accurately pinpointing exact terminology sought after resolving ambiguity surrounding nature mechanism behind complex phenomenon occurring regularly observable everyday existence humanity."
``` Summary: Additional energy breaks the attractions between water molecules when converting boiling water to steam; this energy is called latent heat since adding it doesn't raise the temperature afterward. Option A, 'Latent Heat of Vaporisation,' accurately defines the amount of heat required for this phase change. ```
